Lincang.risk: State-affiliated operator. Subject to export licensing.
Lincang.role: One of the world's highest-grade germanium deposits. Ge was concentrated by hydrothermal fluids from the Lincang granite batholith into ancient swamp coal seams.
Lincang.inv: No direct public vehicle. Exposure via Lincang Xinyuan (002428.SZ).
risks: Chinese export controls limit westward flow
Lowest-cost Ge source globally
Single operator concentration risk

Wulantuga.risk: Only ~17% of theoretical Ge content is actually recovered.
Wulantuga.role: Largest coal-hosted Ge deposit by reserve size in Inner Mongolia. Only ~15-25% of theoretical Ge content is recovered due to specialist extraction requirements.
Wulantuga.inv: Indirect exposure via Shengli Coal Group (state-owned).
risks: Low recovery rate limits supply upside
State ownership means output is policy-driven
No western investment access

Yimin.risk: Lower grade makes extraction marginal at current prices.
Yimin.role: Largest coal-hosted Ge reserve in China by total size but lowest average grade. An optionality play rather than current supply source.
Yimin.inv: Future optionality if Ge prices sustain above $3,000/kg.
risks: Grade too low at current prices
2-3 years to ramp if prices spike
State control limits western access

Huize.risk: Ge recovered only as byproduct — volume is price-inelastic.
Huize.role: One of the highest-grade zinc deposits globally. Germanium is a trace impurity that concentrates during zinc hydrometallurgy.
risks: Ge output tied to zinc production decisions
Zinc downturn reduces Ge supply
Chinese listed - limited western access

Yiliang + SYGT.risk: Fragmented ownership across the SYGT triangle.
Yiliang + SYGT.role: China's richest zinc province. Multiple deposits feed the Qujing smelter system. Ge is extracted as a byproduct of regional zinc processing.
risks: Regional concentration risk
Ownership fragmentation
Full Ge reserves not publicly disclosed

Lincang Xinyuan.role: The only fully vertically integrated coal-route Ge producer. Controls its own mines, power plant, fly-ash leach circuits, and refinery all the way through to solar-grade wafers.
Lincang Xinyuan.inv: Listed on Shenzhen Exchange. Revenue ~CNY 767M (2024). A-share constraints limit western access.
risks: US export ban cuts western revenue
A-share listing limits western investors
Single-site concentration risk

Shengli Coal Group.risk: State-owned enterprise. Output decisions are policy-driven.
Shengli Coal Group.role: Operates the Wulantuga coalfield primarily for power generation. Ge recovery is secondary — fly ash is leached with sulfuric acid to recover Ge concentrate.
Shengli Coal Group.inv: No listed equity. State-owned with no direct western investment vehicle.
risks: Power generation is primary — Ge always secondary
Policy shifts directly impact output
No western investment access

Various State Ops.risk: Fragmented state operators. Output data is opaque.
Various State Ops.role: A collection of smaller state-affiliated operators working Yimin and other lower-grade deposits. Activity rises and falls with Ge price.
risks: Opaque operations with no public reporting
Output highly variable
Exit risk at low prices

Yunnan Chihong.risk: Ge is a byproduct — tied to zinc economics.
Yunnan Chihong.role: China's largest germanium producer by volume. Every tonne of zinc processed carries trace Ge that concentrates into leach residues. Chihong built dedicated circuits to capture this high-margin byproduct.
Yunnan Chihong.inv: Listed on Shanghai Exchange. Ge segment is high-margin relative to total revenue.
risks: Ge output tied to zinc production
Export controls limit western sales
Zinc price weakness reduces Ge output

Lincang Xinyuan Refinery.risk: US export ban directly impacts western revenue.
Lincang Xinyuan Refinery.role: Refines Ge concentrate all the way through to finished products including solar-grade wafers — the only facility with full vertical integration from coal mine to wafer.
Lincang Xinyuan Refinery.inv: Part of Lincang Xinyuan (002428.SZ). Wafer business commands significant premiums over commodity Ge.
risks: US export ban cuts wafer sales to western satellite manufacturers
Single-facility risk
Trade policy exposure

Smaller Chinese Refiners.role: A fragmented group of smaller processors refining Ge concentrate from coal-route sources. Most price-sensitive in the chain — they enter and exit based on spot prices.
Smaller Chinese Refiners.inv: No direct investment access. ~10% of Chinese primary Ge output.
risks: High exit risk when prices fall
No quality standardization
Opaque output reporting

Yunnan Chihong Refinery.risk: Largest single Ge refinery globally. High concentration risk.
Yunnan Chihong Refinery.role: The world's largest single germanium refinery. Processes zinc leach residues from the SYGT belt into refined GeO₂ and metal.
Yunnan Chihong Refinery.inv: Part of Yunnan Chihong (600497.SH). Ge refining contributes disproportionate margins relative to its size within the zinc business.
risks: Single-facility concentration
Export control compliance adds cost
Dependent on zinc smelter economics

China Supply.risk: Subject to export controls and political risk.
China Supply.role: Aggregates all Chinese primary refined germanium before it enters the international supply chain as GeO₂, GeCl₄, or Ge wafers.
China Supply.inv: No direct vehicle. Invest in western companies that benefit from the supply squeeze: Umicore, 5N Plus, Corning.
risks: Export controls can throttle this node at any time
Political escalation risk
No western price discovery mechanism

Umicore GeCl4.stat: World's leading GeCl₄ supplier · western market dominant
Umicore GeCl4.risk: DRC supply ramp risk. Export control dependency on recycled scrap feed.
Umicore GeCl4.role: Umicore is the world's leading supplier of ultrapure GeCl₄ for the telecom optical fiber market, supplying the majority of western fiber manufacturers including Corning, Prysmian, and Fujikura. GeCl₄ is produced by chlorinating GeO₂ from recycled fiber manufacturing scrap and primary concentrate. Purity reaches 8N (99.999999%) — required to minimize signal attenuation in single-mode fiber. During fiber production a large portion of germanium is lost in waste streams — Umicore recovers this scrap and refines it back into GeCl₄, creating a closed-loop tolling system with most major western fiber manufacturers.
Umicore GeCl4.inv: UMI is the most direct western investment vehicle for germanium supply chain exposure. Chinese export controls structurally improve Umicore's pricing power.
stats: Scrap recovery loop with Corning, Prysmian
risks: DRC Big Hill ramp timeline uncertain
Recycled scrap feed shrinks if Chinese fiber output declines
Single facility concentration risk — Olen, Belgium

Yunnan Chihong GeCl4.stat: Largest Chinese GeCl₄ producer · ~66 t Ge/yr refined
Yunnan Chihong GeCl4.risk: Subject to Chinese export controls. Full US export ban since December 2024.
Yunnan Chihong GeCl4.role: Yunnan Chihong is China's largest integrated germanium producer, refining germanium from zinc smelting byproduct and producing GeCl₄ for domestic fiber manufacturers including YOFC. Subject to Chinese export controls — GeCl₄ exports to western markets require licensing approval since August 2023, with a full ban to the US imposed December 2024.
Yunnan Chihong GeCl4.inv: Chinese A-share listed. Indirect proxy for Chinese germanium pricing. No direct western investment vehicle.
risks: Export controls prevent western market access
Price manipulation risk from Chinese state policy
Geopolitical escalation could trigger further restrictions

Chinese State GeCl4 Plants.stat: ~50-60 t combined · Lincang Xinyuan · smaller operators
Chinese State GeCl4 Plants.risk: Export controls block western access. Aggregated opacity.
Chinese State GeCl4 Plants.role: Aggregated node representing smaller Chinese GeCl₄ producers including Lincang Xinyuan Refinery and other state-affiliated operators. Supply primarily domestic fiber manufacturers. Subject to export controls. Combined output feeds China's significant domestic fiber optic manufacturing base — China produces ~40% of global optical fiber.
Chinese State GeCl4 Plants.inv: No direct western investment vehicle. Exposure only via Chinese state-owned enterprise listings.
risks: Export controls block all western supply
Aggregated opacity — actual output uncertain
State directive risk could redirect supply to strategic uses

JSC Germanium GeCl4.risk: Fully sanctioned. Zero western market access since 2022.
JSC Germanium GeCl4.role: JSC Germanium historically supplied GeCl₄ to European and Asian fiber manufacturers. Capacity of ~20t Ge equivalent/yr. Post-2022 Russia sanctions have cut off all western market access. Now serves only the Russian domestic fiber market and potentially Chinese buyers through non-sanctioned channels.
JSC Germanium GeCl4.inv: No investment case. Sanctioned entity. Historically removed ~9% of western-accessible germanium supply.
risks: Fully sanctioned — no western access
Permanently removed from western supply picture
Cannot serve as emergency alternative

Corning.stat: ~25-30% global fiber · largest western Ge consumer
Corning.risk: AI demand backlog — multi-year order book.
Corning.role: Corning's cable division assembles the world's highest-density fiber optic cables for AI datacenters. Enterprise (datacenter) sales grew 58% YoY in Q3 2025. Meta's $6B agreement reserves a significant portion of Corning's global output. Corning's Springboard plan targets 30% CAGR for its enterprise division through AI network growth. Designs include 1,728+ fiber count cables for GPU cluster interconnect.
Corning.inv: GLW is the most liquid western proxy for AI infrastructure fiber demand. Multi-year datacenter backlogs give rare earnings visibility.
risks: GeO₂/GeCl₄ supply constraint limits capacity
Significant China revenue exposure (~$1B+ annually)
Overcapacity risk if datacenter buildout slows

Prysmian.risk: European supply chain exposure. High datacenter exposure.
Prysmian.role: Prysmian is the world's largest cable manufacturer, producing over 30M km/yr across 27 plants in telecom, datacenter, and submarine segments. Largest global market share in fiber cable at >18%. Expanding capacity to meet AI datacenter demand but constrained by 18-24 month preform lead time.
Prysmian.inv: PRY offers terrestrial and subsea exposure. Subsea demand is driven by AI hyperscalers building private subsea networks.
risks: More exposed to European telecom capex cycles
Italian listing reduces liquidity for US investors
Currency risk (EUR-denominated)

Fujikura.risk: Japanese supply chain — limited western direct investment.
Fujikura.role: Fujikura assembles fiber optic cables primarily for the Asia-Pacific market through its direct operations and via OFS Fitel in the US. Produces specialty cables for datacenter, telecom, and utility applications. Key supplier to Japanese and Southeast Asian telecom operators.
Fujikura.inv: TSE-listed with limited western investor access. Asian AI datacenter buildout is a regional demand driver.
risks: TSE listing limits western access
Yen depreciation impacts USD returns
More telecom-weighted than datacenter-weighted

Sumitomo Electric.risk: Diversified across wiring harnesses — fiber is one segment.
Sumitomo Electric.role: Sumitomo Electric produces fiber optic cables for telecom, datacenter, and specialty applications globally. Pioneer in next-generation multi-core and ultra-low-loss fiber cable designs. Set world record 1.02 Pbit/s transmission Jan 2025 using 19-core fiber — next-gen cable types requiring higher germanium precision.
Sumitomo Electric.inv: Unique dual exposure: AI fiber demand and EV wiring demand.
risks: Fiber is a minority of revenue
Auto wiring harness slowdown could weigh on valuation
TSE listing limits western access

Shin-Etsu.stat: Major preform supplier · silicon + Ge dual chemistry
Shin-Etsu.risk: Dual exposure: fiber optics + semiconductor wafers.
Shin-Etsu.role: Shin-Etsu Chemical is a major fiber optic preform manufacturer with unique dual expertise in both silicon and germanium chemistry — it supplies both SiO₂ cladding and GeO₂ core materials. This vertical integration in glass chemistry makes Shin-Etsu less exposed to GeCl₄ supply volatility than pure fiber drawers. Supplies preforms to third-party fiber manufacturers.
Shin-Etsu.inv: Unique dual-exposure play: AI chip demand drives silicon wafer volumes AND AI datacenter buildout drives fiber preform volumes.
risks: Silicon wafer oversupply risk if AI chip demand cycles
Japanese listed — limited western direct access
Yen exposure for USD investors

YOFC.stat: Largest in China · ~10% global · Corning JV
YOFC.risk: Chinese state-backed. Partial Corning JV. Geopolitically complex.
YOFC.role: YOFC is China's largest fiber manufacturer and among the top globally. State-controlled with Corning as JV partner — giving access to western preform technology while retaining direct access to domestic Chinese GeCl₄ unaffected by export controls. In November 2023 began commercial production of hollow-core fiber at scale. China's domestic fiber manufacturers are insulated from export control risk — they source GeCl₄ from Yunnan Chihong and state chemical plants.
YOFC.inv: Chinese listed A-share with limited western access. The Corning JV structure creates a direct link between Chinese Ge supply and western fiber manufacturing IP.
risks: Geopolitical risk — Chinese state ownership
Corning JV creates IP transfer concerns
Export control risk limits western sales potential

Optical Fiber Strand.stat: ~500M fiber-km/yr current · ~1.3B km needed for AI by 2026
Optical Fiber Strand.risk: Supply cannot triple in time without proportional GeCl₄ increase.
Optical Fiber Strand.role: Global optical fiber strand production is currently ~500 million fiber-km per year. AI datacenter buildout is driving demand toward ~1.3 billion fiber-km per year by 2026 — a 2.6x increase requiring a proportional increase in GeCl₄ supply. Each fiber-km contains approximately 0.02-0.05g of germanium in the core. At 1.3B km annual deployment, fiber alone would require ~180 tonnes of germanium per year — against total global supply of ~220 tonnes.
Optical Fiber Strand.inv: Do not invest at the strand level. Invest upstream at GeCl₄ suppliers or downstream at hyperscale operators.
stats: 180 t Ge/yr for fiber alone vs 220 t total
risks: Supply cannot triple without proportional GeCl₄ increase
Chinese export controls directly constrain capacity expansion
Demand pull-forward could exhaust western GeCl₄ stocks

Vertiv.risk: AI density driving unprecedented thermal load per rack.
stats: GPU clusters require 5-10x more cooling than CPUs
Vertiv.role: Vertiv makes the power distribution, uninterruptible power supply, and thermal management systems that every datacenter depends on. AI GPU clusters consume 40-100kW per rack versus 5-10kW for traditional compute — Vertiv is the primary beneficiary of this density explosion.
Vertiv.inv: VRT is arguably the purest AI infrastructure play outside of Nvidia. Every GPU cluster needs Vertiv power and cooling systems. Revenue growing >20% annually driven entirely by AI datacenter buildout.
risks: Backlog-driven revenue creates lumpy delivery timing
High valuations leave limited margin of safety
Supply chain constraints on transformers and cooling components

Equinix.risk: AI workloads require owned capacity, not colo — structural headwind.
Equinix.role: Equinix provides colocation space, power, and physical interconnection between networks. The Equinix fabric connects 10,000+ networks globally — it is the physical internet exchange infrastructure that routes traffic between cloud providers, enterprises, and content networks.
Equinix.inv: EQIX is a REIT with stable recurring revenue. Less direct AI exposure than Vertiv — hyperscalers prefer to own their AI training facilities rather than colocate them. The interconnection business (charging for cross-connects between networks) is durable and high-margin.
risks: Hyperscalers increasingly building their own infrastructure rather than using colo
AI training clusters prefer owned facilities for power density control
Interest rate sensitivity as a REIT with significant debt

CoreWeave.risk: Highly concentrated customer base. Microsoft is ~60% of revenue.
CoreWeave.role: CoreWeave is an AI-native cloud built entirely around GPU compute. Unlike AWS or Azure which offer GPUs as a small fraction of their portfolio, CoreWeave's entire infrastructure is optimized for AI training and inference. Rents GPU capacity to AI companies and enterprises that cannot get enough capacity from hyperscalers.
CoreWeave.inv: CRWV IPO'd in March 2025. The only pure-play GPU cloud stock. Extraordinary growth but extreme customer concentration risk with Microsoft as dominant customer.
risks: ~60% revenue from Microsoft creates existential concentration risk
Massive debt load from GPU purchases (~$8B+)
Nvidia dependency — entire business model depends on Nvidia GPU supply

Schneider Electric.risk: Less pure-play than Vertiv — diversified industrial conglomerate.
Schneider Electric.role: Schneider Electric makes the switchgear, PDUs, DCIM software, and prefabricated datacenter modules that sit upstream of servers in every datacenter power chain. Also operates EcoStruxure — a datacenter management platform monitoring power and cooling efficiency.
Schneider Electric.inv: SU offers European-listed AI infrastructure exposure with a more diversified risk profile than Vertiv. Slower growth but more stable. The prefabricated datacenter module business is a strong AI tailwind.
risks: Diversified revenue base dilutes AI datacenter growth contribution
European listing and currency risk for USD investors
Competition from Vertiv and Eaton in core power distribution market

AECOM.risk: Construction is cyclical. Revenue recognized on project completion.
AECOM.role: AECOM designs and manages construction of hyperscale datacenter campuses — the physical buildings, electrical infrastructure, cooling systems, and site development that house AI compute. Every major new hyperscale campus requires engineering firms like AECOM for permitting, design, and construction management.
AECOM.inv: ACM is an indirect AI play through datacenter construction boom. Multi-billion dollar hyperscale buildouts create sustained engineering demand. More stable than hardware plays but less direct AI exposure.
risks: Construction revenue is lumpy and project-dependent
Labor and materials cost inflation squeezes margins
Less direct AI exposure than infrastructure equipment makers

Microsoft.risk: Massive AI capex commitment. $80B+ in datacenter spend (2025).
Microsoft.role: Microsoft is the largest single consumer of fiber optic cable and datacenter infrastructure globally. Its exclusive cloud relationship with OpenAI makes Azure the primary deployment platform for GPT-4 and successors. The Copilot integration across Office 365 (1.4B users) creates the largest AI inference workload in existence.
Microsoft.inv: MSFT is the most accessible AI infrastructure play at the hyperscale level. The OpenAI relationship creates durable AI revenue moats. Trading at ~32x forward earnings — premium but justified by AI growth trajectory.
risks: OpenAI relationship is non-exclusive at the model level
Azure losing market share to AWS in some enterprise segments
Regulatory scrutiny of OpenAI partnership in EU and UK

Google.risk: Search revenue under threat from AI-native search alternatives.
Google.role: Google pioneered large-scale AI infrastructure. Its Tensor Processing Units (TPUs) are purpose-built AI accelerators that rival Nvidia GPUs at scale. DeepMind and Google Brain merged into Google DeepMind — the world's most published AI research organization. GCP is the third-largest cloud platform globally.
Google.inv: GOOGL offers AI exposure plus the most diversified revenue base of any hyperscaler — search, YouTube, GCP, hardware. The TPU custom silicon strategy reduces Nvidia dependency and improves margins at scale.
risks: Search revenue disruption from AI-native search (Perplexity, ChatGPT Search)
Antitrust scrutiny on search monopoly
GCP continues to lag AWS and Azure in enterprise market share

Amazon.risk: AWS AI revenue growing but Bedrock faces stiff competition.
Amazon.role: AWS is the world's largest cloud platform and the primary infrastructure layer for thousands of AI startups. Amazon Bedrock provides API access to models from Anthropic, Meta, Mistral, and others. Trainium chips are purpose-built for AI training at AWS scale.
Amazon.inv: AMZN offers AI exposure plus the strongest e-commerce and logistics moats of any tech company. AWS is ~75% of Amazon operating income. The Anthropic investment (~$4B) provides strategic AI model access.
risks: Bedrock's multi-model strategy may commoditize AI API revenue
Trainium chip adoption slower than hoped vs Nvidia GPUs
Retail segment margins remain thin despite logistics improvements

Meta.risk: Advertising revenue concentration. AI capex massive relative to AI revenue.
Meta.role: Meta is the largest single builder of AI training infrastructure by GPU count. The Llama open-source strategy is a deliberate attempt to commoditize AI models and reduce competitive moats. AI powers Meta's ad targeting (generating ~$160B revenue) and new consumer products (Meta AI assistant).
Meta.inv: META trades at the lowest multiple of the major hyperscalers despite strong AI monetization through ads. Llama open-source strategy is misunderstood — Meta benefits from ecosystem development while retaining infrastructure advantages.
risks: AI capex growing faster than AI revenue
Regulatory risk in EU on data privacy
Advertiser concentration and macroeconomic sensitivity of ad spend

xAI.risk: Private company. No direct investment vehicle currently.
xAI.role: xAI built the Colossus supercomputer in Memphis in 122 days — the fastest large-scale GPU cluster deployment in history. Grok 3 is competitive with GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 on benchmarks. Deep integration with X (Twitter) provides unique real-time data advantage for training.
xAI.inv: No direct investment vehicle. Monitor for IPO. Indirect exposure via Nvidia (GPU supplier) and Tesla (Elon relationship and shared compute resources). Series C at ~$50B valuation implies significant upside if Grok achieves meaningful enterprise adoption.
risks: No direct investment access
Elon Musk concentration risk across Tesla, SpaceX, X, xAI simultaneously
Competing against Microsoft+OpenAI, Google, Amazon with deeper resources

Oracle.risk: Late cloud entrant. OCI growing fast but from small base.
Oracle.role: Oracle is the fastest-growing major cloud platform driven entirely by AI infrastructure demand. xAI chose OCI for Colossus overflow capacity. The US government's $500B Stargate initiative includes Oracle as a primary infrastructure partner alongside Microsoft and SoftBank. Oracle's database dominance in enterprise creates a natural migration path to OCI.
Oracle.inv: ORCL is the most underappreciated AI infrastructure play among large-cap tech. OCI growing faster than any major cloud platform. Stargate partnership with US government creates durable, long-term contracted revenue.
risks: OCI starting from a much smaller base than AWS, Azure, GCP
Database licensing revenue declining as enterprises migrate to cloud
Stargate project timelines and funding are uncertain

AI Datacenter.stat: The culmination of the Ge supply chain
AI Datacenter.risk: Demand growing faster than supply chain can support.
AI Datacenter.role: The AI datacenter is where the entire germanium supply chain terminates. Every fiber optic cable connecting GPU to GPU, switch to switch, and datacenter to datacenter began as germanium dioxide in a Chinese coal seam or zinc mine. A single hyperscale AI training cluster may contain millions of kilometers of fiber — representing thousands of kilograms of germanium permanently embedded in glass.
AI Datacenter.inv: At the end use layer, invest in the operators and infrastructure suppliers — not the fiber or cable itself. The value of connected AI infrastructure accrues to the compute owners (Microsoft, Google, Amazon, Meta) and the infrastructure enablers (Vertiv, Schneider, CoreWeave). The Ge supply chain is upstream leverage on this entire ecosystem.
risks: AI compute demand could slow if model efficiency improves faster than scale
Power grid constraints limiting datacenter buildout in key markets
Geopolitical risk to fiber supply chain (Chinese Ge export controls) creates cost headwinds for all operators

Fiber Optic Cable.risk: Supply cannot 2.6× before 2027. Ge dependency in every meter.
Fiber Optic Cable.role: Global fiber optic cable output is currently ~500 million cable-km per year across all types — datacenter, terrestrial, and submarine. AI datacenter demand alone is projected to require ~1.3 billion fiber-km by 2026, representing a 2.6x increase over current total capacity. The gap cannot be closed before late 2027 due to the 18-24 month preform expansion lag. Fiber prices have surged 500%+ since January 2025 as the market reprices the structural shortage. The germanium dependency runs through every meter of this cable — each km of single-mode fiber contains trace germanium in its core, and the entire global supply chain depends on ~220 tonnes of refined germanium per year.
Fiber Optic Cable.inv: The cable itself is a commodity. Invest in manufacturers with highest datacenter/subsea mix (Corning, Prysmian) or at the hyperscale operator level.
risks: Supply cannot triple without proportional GeCl₄ increase
Chinese export controls directly constrain capacity expansion
Demand pull-forward could exhaust western GeCl₄ stocks

Datacenter Cable.risk: Prices surged 500%+ in 2025–2026 on AI demand.
Datacenter Cable.role: Datacenter fiber optic cable connects GPU clusters, spine-leaf switching networks, and datacenter interconnects. AI datacenters require 36x more fiber than traditional CPU racks — a single million-GPU cluster can consume tens of thousands of fiber-km. High fiber count cables (1,728+ strands) are now standard for hyperscale AI builds. Prices surged 500%+ in 2025-2026 driven by AI demand. Meta's $6B Corning deal and Lumen's Corning agreement represent the new norm of multi-year locked supply.
Datacenter Cable.inv: Invest in manufacturers with the highest datacenter revenue mix: Corning, CommScope.
risks: Commoditization risk as manufacturing scales
Hyperscaler in-sourcing risk
Demand tied entirely to datacenter capex cycles

Terrestrial Long-Haul.stat: 12 to 432 fibers/cable · 213M fiber-miles needed US by 2029
Terrestrial Long-Haul.role: Long-haul terrestrial fiber cables carry internet backbone traffic between cities and datacenters. Standard single-mode fiber (G.652D) is the dominant cable type. US needs to add 213.3 million fiber miles by 2029 to support AI datacenter interconnect demands — more than doubling current installed base. 5G transport backhaul adds additional demand on top of existing FTTH and backbone replacement cycles.
Terrestrial Long-Haul.inv: More telecom-dependent than datacenter cable — slower growth but more stable recurring demand.
risks: Telecom operator capex cycles create demand volatility
5G backhaul buildout varies by country
Competition from wireless backhaul alternatives

Subsea Cable.stat: 8 to 24 fiber pairs · ~40 new systems entering service 2026
Subsea Cable.risk: Geopolitical risk around cable routes and landing rights.
Subsea Cable.role: Submarine fiber optic cables carry ~99% of international internet traffic across ocean floors. Nearly 40 new submarine cable systems enter service in 2026 alone — the most active year in history, worth ~$6B in construction. Hyperscalers (Google, Meta, Microsoft, Amazon) now fund private submarine cables directly, bypassing traditional telecom carriers. SubCom, Alcatel Submarine Networks, and NEC collectively control >60% of wet-plant revenue. Each system uses Ge-doped single-mode fiber for the entire cable length.
Subsea Cable.inv: AI hyperscalers are funding private subsea cable projects. This structural demand shift benefits Alcatel SN and SubCom most directly.
risks: Geopolitical tension around cable landing rights
Repair costs and lead times are extremely high
Route concentration through a handful of landing stations

Alcatel SN.risk: Recently transferred to French state ownership — geopolitical asset.
Alcatel SN.role: Alcatel Submarine Networks is one of three companies controlling over 60% of global submarine cable wet-plant revenue. Recently transferred to French state ownership — underscoring governments' heightened interest in controlling critical subsea infrastructure. Partners with hyperscalers (Google, Meta, Microsoft, Amazon) building private submarine cable systems. Signed partnership with leading cloud provider for next-generation submarine cable system in July 2025.
Alcatel SN.inv: Exposure via Nokia (NOKIA HE) prior to divestiture. Now French state asset. AI hyperscalers are funding private subsea cables directly, driving unprecedented demand.
risks: Geopolitical risk around cable routes and landing rights
Subsea projects have 3-5 year lead times — lumpy revenue
State ownership may affect commercial flexibility

SubCom.role: SubCom is the leading US submarine cable system supplier, combining cable manufacturing with marine installation vessels — a critical vertical integration that limits competition. SubCom controls a major share of US government-approved submarine cable projects given national security scrutiny of Chinese competitors. Expanding marine fulfillment capabilities to meet surging demand from 40 new submarine cable systems entering service in 2026.
SubCom.inv: No direct investment — private Cerberus portfolio company. An IPO or strategic sale would be significant given AI-driven subsea demand.
risks: Private — no investment access
Revenue concentration in large contracts
US government scrutiny of subsea cable routes

NEC.risk: Subsea is a minority of NEC's total revenue.
NEC.role: NEC Corporation has deployed over 400,000 km of undersea cables worldwide, making it one of the most experienced submarine cable installers globally. Dominant in the Asia-Pacific submarine cable market. Unveiled new submarine cable system for Asia in September 2025 to meet growing data traffic. Competes with SubCom and Alcatel Submarine Networks for hyperscaler private cable projects.
NEC.inv: NEC is a diversified Japanese IT company — subsea cable is important but not dominant. Indirect play via TSE listing.
stats: New system for Asian AI traffic
risks: Subsea is minority of revenue
TSE listing limits western access
Japanese government IT services is the primary business

CommScope.stat: CCS segment +27% YoY Q4 2024 · Propel 800G platform
CommScope.role: CommScope's Connectivity & Cable Solutions segment is the fastest-growing division, driven by hyperscale and cloud datacenter GenAI buildout. Q4 2024 net sales +27% YoY with CCS leading all segments. Launched Propel modular fiber platform in 2024 enabling 400G and 800G datacenter applications, reducing installation time by 30%. Strong US manufacturing presence for BABA-compliant broadband infrastructure.
CommScope.inv: COMM is a more leveraged play on US datacenter infrastructure buildout. Higher risk than Corning but also higher upside if datacenter spending continues.
risks: $9B+ debt from 2019 ARRIS acquisition
Not vertically integrated — exposed to strand price fluctuations
Slower to benefit from Ge supply constraint than upstream players

AFL.risk: Private company — no direct investment vehicle.
AFL.role: AFL (Fujikura subsidiary) is a major US cable assembler focused on utility, datacenter, and enterprise fiber optic infrastructure. Produces specialty cables for power utilities, military, and hyperscale datacenter applications. Fully US-based manufacturing makes AFL a BABA-compliant domestic supplier for government-funded broadband and defense programs.
AFL.inv: No direct investment — privately held Fujikura subsidiary. Indirect exposure via Fujikura (5803 TSE).
risks: No listed equity vehicle
Dependent on Fujikura parent strategy
US utility cable market has different demand cycle than datacenter

Umicore.stat: World's largest Ge recycler · UMI Brussels
Umicore.risk: Feed dependent on western manufacturing scrap — constrained by fiber optic production volumes.
Umicore.role: Umicore is the western world's primary germanium recycler and refiner, operating out of Hoboken near Antwerp. More than 50% of their germanium feed comes from recycling customer scrap — fiber optic manufacturing waste from Corning and Prysmian, spent infrared optics, and decommissioned military equipment. Umicore is the strategic western buffer against Chinese export controls.
Umicore.inv: UMI offers the most direct western exposure to germanium supply security. As China's export restrictions tighten, Umicore's recycling model becomes structurally more valuable — it sources scrap domestically from western fiber manufacturers, entirely bypassing Chinese primary supply. The 2024 DRC partnership adds primary concentrate feed for the first time.
risks: Germanium output volumes not publicly disclosed — estimates only
Feed volume constrained by western fiber optic manufacturing scrap availability
DRC partnership (Chemaf/STL) adds geopolitical risk from a different direction

5N Plus.stat: US DoD strategic partner · VNP TSX
5N Plus.risk: Small scale relative to Umicore and Chinese producers.
5N Plus.role: 5N Plus produces high-purity germanium products including wafers for satellite solar cells and semiconductor applications. The US Department of Defense partnered with 5N Plus to establish a germanium wafer production facility in Utah — a direct strategic response to Chinese export controls on germanium announced in 2023.
5N Plus.inv: VNP is the most direct investment play on US germanium supply independence. The DoD partnership provides contracted revenue and de-risks the business model. As US defense and space programs require domestically sourced germanium wafers, 5N Plus is structurally positioned as the primary American supplier.
risks: Small scale — cannot meaningfully replace Chinese primary supply
DoD revenue concentration creates customer dependency
Dependent on imported germanium feed — not fully vertically integrated

PPM Pure Metals.risk: Private company — limited transparency on volumes and financials.
PPM Pure Metals.role: PPM Pure Metals is a German specialty refiner focused on ultra-high purity germanium for semiconductor and detector applications. Their zone refining process achieves 7N+ purity (99.99999%+) — the highest grade required for radiation detection and advanced semiconductor applications. Supplies European defense and research institutions.
PPM Pure Metals.inv: No direct investment vehicle — private company. Indirect exposure via European critical minerals supply chain security initiatives. PPM represents the European strategic refining capability for defense-grade germanium.
risks: Private — no investment access
Small scale, niche market focus
Dependent on scrap feed from European fiber and IR optics manufacturers

China Primary Supply.risk: Export controls implemented August 2023 — ongoing supply risk for western buyers.
China Primary Supply.role: China Primary Supply represents the aggregated output of Chinese germanium refiners — Lincang Xinyuan Refinery, Yunnan Chihong Refinery, and smaller operators. This is primary germanium refined from coal fly ash and zinc smelting byproducts. China implemented export licensing requirements for germanium in August 2023, creating structural uncertainty for all western buyers.
China Primary Supply.inv: China's dominance of primary germanium supply is the central investment thesis of this supply chain. The export control regime has bifurcated the market — Chinese buyers get unrestricted access while western buyers face licensing delays and price premiums. This creates durable demand for western recycling alternatives.
risks: Export licensing can be tightened or revoked at any time
Chinese domestic demand consuming growing share of output
No western alternative at equivalent scale

Western Recycled Supply.risk: Volume constrained by western manufacturing scrap — cannot scale independently.
Western Recycled Supply.role: Western Recycled Supply aggregates germanium recovered by Umicore, 5N Plus, PPM Pure Metals, and smaller recyclers from manufacturing scrap — primarily fiber optic production waste from Corning, Prysmian, and Fujikura. This stream is the western world's strategic buffer against Chinese primary supply disruption.
Western Recycled Supply.inv: The growth of western recycled supply is structurally driven by China's export controls. Every tonne recycled by Umicore or 5N Plus is a tonne that doesn't need to be sourced from China. As AI datacenter buildout drives fiber optic manufacturing volumes, the scrap stream feeding western recyclers also grows — a natural tailwind.
risks: Cannot scale beyond available manufacturing scrap volumes
Still partially dependent on Chinese primary Ge for high-purity applications
Western recycling capacity remains well below Chinese primary output

Global Supply.risk: China controls ~57% of primary supply with active export restrictions.
Global Supply.role: Global germanium supply combines Chinese primary refining (~120t/yr) and western recycling (~90-110t/yr). Total supply is structurally constrained — germanium cannot be mined directly and supply growth requires either increased coal/zinc processing in China or expanded western recycling capacity. The 2023 Chinese export controls have permanently bifurcated the market.
Global Supply.inv: The global supply constraint is the investment thesis. ~220t/yr of supply serving rapidly growing AI infrastructure, defense, and solar demand. Supply cannot respond quickly to demand increases — it is locked to coal combustion and zinc smelting rates in China, and to western fiber manufacturing scrap volumes. Price is the release valve.
risks: Supply permanently capped by byproduct dependency
Chinese export controls create ongoing western supply risk
Recycled supply cannot scale fast enough to close the gap if China tightens controls further

Spetsugli.risk: Output blocked from western markets post-2022 Russia sanctions.
Spetsugli.role: Spetsugli is the third of the world's three active coal-hosted germanium deposits, alongside Lincang and Wulantuga. Located in the Pavlovsk Coalfield in Russia's Far East, it has been mined since the 1960s. JSC Germanium extracts germanium from coal fly ash at the site and refines it at a facility in Krasnoyarsk. Historically exported 80%+ of output to western markets — that flow has been effectively cut off since 2022.
Spetsugli.inv: No direct investment vehicle. Spetsugli represents ~10% of theoretical global germanium supply that is structurally unavailable to western buyers due to sanctions. Its exclusion from western supply chains permanently tightens the accessible supply picture and strengthens the investment case for Umicore and 5N Plus as the primary western alternatives.
risks: Post-2022 sanctions effectively block all western access to Russian germanium
JSC Germanium is part of Rostec, a sanctioned state defense conglomerate
Sanctions could be reversed but political timeline is entirely unpredictable

Big Hill.stat: 14M t slag · 700+ t Ge potential · ramping up
Big Hill.risk: DRC political and operational risk. Volume unproven at scale.
Big Hill.role: Big Hill is not a geological deposit — it is a century of accumulated mining slag from Lubumbashi's copper and cobalt industry. STL, a subsidiary of DRC state miner Gécamines, processes the slag through a new hydrometallurgical plant to extract germanium concentrate, which is then shipped exclusively to Umicore in Belgium for final refining. First shipment occurred October 2024. If fully ramped, could supply up to 30% of global germanium demand.
Big Hill.inv: Big Hill is the most significant emerging non-Chinese germanium source in the world. The exclusive offtake agreement with Umicore makes UMI the primary beneficiary of any ramp-up. No direct investment in STL or Gécamines is possible for most western investors — exposure is via Umicore (UMI). The DRC has 700+ tonnes of estimated germanium in the slag heap — at current prices that represents $2B+ of embedded value.
risks: DRC political risk — Gécamines is a state-owned entity subject to government policy changes
Hydrometallurgical plant is new and unproven at scale — ramp-up timeline uncertain
Exclusive Umicore offtake means no price competition — Umicore controls the western refining bottleneck

Red Dog.stat: World's largest zinc mine · Ge byproduct · mine life to 2031
Red Dog.risk: Declining grades — production falling each year. Mine life ends 2031.
Red Dog.role: Red Dog is the world's largest zinc mine, located 170km north of the Arctic Circle in northwest Alaska on land owned by the NANA Regional Corporation. Germanium is recovered as a byproduct of zinc concentrate processing at Teck's Trail metallurgical facility in British Columbia. Germanium output volumes are not publicly disclosed. The mine has entered its decline phase — zinc grades are falling and production is expected to decrease each year through to 2031.
Red Dog.inv: Teck (TECK.B TSX / TECK NYSE) offers indirect germanium exposure but the primary investment thesis is copper — Teck sold its coal business in 2023 and is repositioning as a copper-focused miner. Red Dog's germanium is a byproduct of a declining zinc operation. The more relevant angle is that Red Dog's decline removes one of the few non-Chinese zinc-germanium sources from the global supply picture — a structural tightening of western supply.
risks: Mine life ends 2031 based on current developed deposits
Germanium output volumes not publicly disclosed — no direct tracking
Teck is merging with Anglo American — Red Dog's future ownership and operation post-merger unclear

JSC Germanium.stat: ~20 t/yr capacity · Rostec subsidiary · 80%+ historically exported
JSC Germanium.risk: Sanctioned entity — output inaccessible to western buyers since 2022.
JSC Germanium.role: JSC Germanium operates an integrated germanium refinery in Krasnoyarsk processing coal fly ash from the Spetsugli deposit plus recycled waste streams. Part of the Rostec state defense conglomerate via the Shvabe holding company. Historically exported more than 80% of production to western markets including Europe and Japan. That export flow has been cut off since Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine triggered western sanctions.
JSC Germanium.inv: No investment access — state-owned sanctioned entity. JSC Germanium's exclusion from western supply represents a permanent structural tightening of accessible germanium supply, strengthening the investment case for Umicore and 5N Plus.
risks: Sanctioned entity — no western investment or trade possible
Part of Rostec defense conglomerate — geopolitical risk is structural not cyclical
Production continues but serves only Russia and potentially China

STL / Gécamines.stat: First Ge export Oct 2024 · exclusive Umicore offtake · ramping
STL / Gécamines.risk: DRC political risk. New facility unproven at scale.
STL / Gécamines.role: STL processes the Big Hill slag heap through a new hydrometallurgical plant commissioned in October 2023, extracting germanium concentrate from a century of accumulated copper mining waste. All output ships exclusively to Umicore in Belgium under a long-term exclusive partnership signed May 2024. First export batch shipped October 2024. If fully ramped, could represent the largest new non-Chinese germanium source in the world.
STL / Gécamines.inv: No direct investment vehicle — Gécamines is DRC state-owned. The investment play is Umicore (UMI), which holds the exclusive offtake and benefits directly from every tonne STL produces. This partnership is one of the key reasons UMI is strategically positioned as the western germanium supply hub.
risks: DRC state ownership creates policy and nationalization risk
Hydrometallurgical plant is new — ramp-up timeline and volumes unproven
Exclusive Umicore offtake means no market price competition for this source

Teck Resources.stat: World's largest zinc mine · Ge byproduct · TECK NYSE/TSX
Teck Resources.risk: Red Dog mine life ends 2031. Germanium volumes not disclosed.
Teck Resources.role: Teck operates Red Dog in Alaska, the world's largest zinc mine, producing germanium as an undisclosed byproduct. Zinc concentrates are shipped to Teck's Trail metallurgical complex in British Columbia where germanium is recovered. Teck does not publicly disclose germanium production volumes. The company is in the process of merging with Anglo American and is repositioning as a copper-focused miner.
Teck Resources.inv: TECK offers indirect germanium exposure but copper is the primary thesis. The more relevant germanium angle is that Red Dog's decline removes one of the few non-Chinese zinc-germanium sources — a structural tightening of western supply that benefits Umicore and 5N Plus more than Teck itself.
risks: Red Dog mine life ends 2031 — germanium source is finite and declining
Teck-Anglo American merger may change operational priorities at Red Dog
Germanium revenue is unquantified — no way to track or invest on that basis

JSC Germanium Refinery.risk: Sanctioned — inaccessible to western buyers and investors.
JSC Germanium Refinery.products: Ge metal, GeO₂, GeCl₄, IR optics, wafers
JSC Germanium Refinery.role: JSC Germanium operates a fully integrated refinery in Krasnoyarsk processing Spetsugli fly ash and recycled germanium waste into finished products including germanium metal, dioxide, tetrachloride, IR optical blanks, and wafers. Has launched germanium wafer production for the Russian space industry. Historically the world's second largest germanium exporter after China — now effectively removed from the global market.
risks: Sanctioned — no western trade or investment
Serves Russian domestic and potentially Chinese markets only
Represents structural supply removal from western accessible market
